Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

Patrick Lewis, Ethan Perez, Aleksandra Piktus, Fabio Petroni,
Vladimir Karpukhin, Naman Goyal, Heinrich Kuttler, Mike Lewis,
Wen-tau Yih, Tim Rocktaschel, Sebastian Riedel, Douwe Kiela
Facebook AI Research; University College London; New York University

Abstract

Large pre-trained language models store factual knowledge in their parameters
and achieve strong results when fine-tuned on downstream tasks. However, their
ability to access and precisely manipulate knowledge remains limited, and
performance on knowledge-intensive tasks lags behind task-specific
architectures. These models also struggle to provide decision provenance and
update world knowledge.

We explore a general-purpose fine-tuning approach for retrieval-augmented
generation (RAG) that combines pre-trained parametric and non-parametric memory
for language generation. A pre-trained seq2seq model acts as parametric memory,
and a dense vector index of Wikipedia acts as non-parametric memory, which is
accessed with a pre-trained neural retriever. We compare two formulations: one
where the same retrieved passages are used throughout generation (RAG-Sequence),
and another where different passages can be used for each token (RAG-Token).

We evaluated our models on a wide range of knowledge-intensive NLP tasks. When
fine-tuned on open-domain question-answering, RAG models achieve state-of-the-art
results on three open-domain QA benchmarks, surpassing both parametric seq2seq
models and task-specific retrieve-and-extract architectures. For language
generation tasks, RAG models produce language that is more specific, diverse,
and factual than a parametric-only seq2seq baseline.
